---
title: "Different cells, different models: choosing a modelling paradigm by cell biology"
runningauthor: "Haigh & Fry"
runninghead: "Modelling paradigms and cell biology"
template-type: pnasresearcharticle
classoption:
  - twocolumn
by-author:
  - name:
      literal: "Steve Haigh"
    affiliations:
      - ref: reading
    attributes:
      corresponding: true
    email: "steve.haigh@gmail.com"  # TODO confirm institutional address
  - name:
      literal: "Mike Fry"  # TODO confirm authorship + initials
    affiliations:
      - ref: reading
by-affiliation:
  - id: reading
    name: "School of Biological Sciences"
    department: "University of Reading"
    city: "Reading"
    country: "United Kingdom"
keywords:
  - whole-cell modelling
  - virtual cell
  - foundation models
  - platelet
  - mechanistic biology
significance: |
  The AI virtual-cell programmes that now dominate computational cell
  biology are built on a single methodological wager: that learned
  representations of single-cell transcriptomes will generalise to
  arbitrary cells. That wager pays out well for the cells the field has
  trained on — proliferating, nucleated, transcriptionally active human
  cancer lines. It pays out badly for cells whose biology cuts against
  the dominant validation set: anucleate cells, terminally differentiated
  cells, cells whose interesting dynamics happen on sub-minute
  timescales, and cells for which single-cell data are sparse. By
  foregrounding the cell biology before the modelling technology, we
  argue that mechanistic and hybrid approaches are not historical
  predecessors of AI virtual cells but co-equal paradigms whose use
  should be determined by the cell and the question.
abstract: |
  The current discourse on the AI virtual cell, exemplified by the Chan
  Zuckerberg Initiative's Virtual Cells Platform [@bunne2024build], is
  dominated by transcriptomics foundation models trained and validated
  on dividing, nucleated, well-sampled cell lines such as K562, HEPG2,
  RPE1, and Jurkat. Mechanistic whole-cell modelling [@karr2012; @macklin2020]
  is frequently positioned as a historical predecessor rather than a
  complementary paradigm. We argue that the choice of modelling paradigm
  should be driven by the cell biology and the research question, not
  by technology momentum. We compare three paradigms — mechanistic
  whole-cell models, transcriptomics foundation models, and hybrid
  causal-mechanistic machine learning — on what each does well, what
  each does poorly, and how validation and computational cost differ.
  We use the human platelet — anucleate, terminally differentiated,
  with a circulating lifespan of approximately nine days and signalling
  events on sub-minute timescales — as a worked example that breaks the
  transcriptomics-foundation-model orthodoxy and makes the case for
  mechanistic and hybrid approaches. We close with three pragmatic
  convergences: machine-learning priors for mechanistic parameters,
  mechanistic models as training signal for learned representations,
  and AI surrogates that accelerate mechanistic simulation.

bibliography: references.bib
format:
  pnas-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
---

# Introduction

The phrase *AI virtual cell* (AIVC) has come, in the space of two years,
to dominate the public face of computational cell biology
[@bunne2024build; @cui2025multimodal; @yu2025aivcm]. The Chan Zuckerberg
Initiative's Virtual Cells Platform, the recent crop of single-cell
foundation models [@theodoris2023geneformer; @cui2024scgpt;
@czi2025transcriptformer], and a growing body of position pieces from
both academia and industry [@valence2025; @szalata2024] frame the
virtual cell as a learnable object: a representation, trained on
many millions of single-cell transcriptomes, capable in principle of
generalising across tissue, perturbation, and species. The framing is
seductive, and the engineering achievements are real. It is also a
framing that, almost everywhere it is articulated, assumes a particular
set of cells. The cells in question divide; they are nucleated; their
transcriptional state is informative about their functional state on
the timescale of measurement; and they are abundant enough in public
single-cell atlases that a foundation model has something to fit. K562,
HEPG2, RPE1, Jurkat, and the rest of the proliferating-cancer-line
canon are the implicit referents whenever a virtual-cell paper reports
a benchmark.

This is unobjectionable so long as those cells exhaust the biology one
cares about. They do not. A great deal of cell biology is done outside
the dividing-and-nucleated canon. Terminally differentiated neurons,
cardiomyocytes, red blood cells, and platelets together account for a
substantial fraction of the cells in the human body and a similarly
large fraction of clinically relevant biology, yet none of them fit the
implicit transcriptomics-first frame: neurons and cardiomyocytes are
post-mitotic and exhibit functional dynamics — action potentials,
excitation-contraction coupling — on timescales where transcription is
silent. Red blood cells have no nucleus. Platelets have no nucleus and
no organised transcriptional apparatus to speak of, complete their
biological work over seconds to minutes, and are catastrophically
under-represented in single-cell atlases relative to their numerical
abundance in blood. For these cells, the methodology one chooses is
not a matter of taste; it is forced by the cell.

The argument of this Perspective is that the choice of modelling
paradigm should follow from the cell biology and the research
question, not from the prevailing technology. We make that argument by
contrasting three modelling paradigms now in active use, locating each
on a small set of axes that capture the practical trade-offs between
them, and then working through the human platelet as a case in which
the dominant transcriptomics-foundation-model paradigm breaks down and
mechanistic and hybrid approaches succeed.

# Three modelling paradigms

## Mechanistic whole-cell models

Mechanistic whole-cell modelling, exemplified by the *Mycoplasma
genitalium* model of @karr2012 and the *Escherichia coli* WCM of
@macklin2020, takes the position that a cell is a system of identifiable
biochemical processes — transcription, translation, replication,
metabolism, signalling — each with a known mechanism and known rate
constants, coupled through a shared state of molecular counts and
concentrations. A typical whole-cell model carries explicit kinetic
equations for thousands of reactions [TODO confirm gene/reaction counts
for Karr 2012 (~525 genes / 28 processes) and Macklin 2020 (~4 500 genes
/ 31 processes)], with simulation proceeding by alternating
process-resource-request, state-partition, and process-evolve steps
under a global timestep. The contracts between processes and shared
state are typed and explicit: any parameter that enters a rate
expression is traceable to a data source through what the wcEcoli
literature calls the *sim_data* mapping [@macklin2020]. Validation
proceeds in two directions, against system-level phenotypes (growth
rate, doubling time, gene-knockout viability) and against subsystem
quantitative behaviour (metabolic flux maps, ribosome occupancy).

What a mechanistic whole-cell model does well is that it forces every
quantity in the model to be either measured, derived, or explicitly
calibrated, and it makes the reasons for a particular simulated
behaviour interpretable in the language of the underlying biology.
What it does badly is scale and data hunger: building a mechanistic
whole-cell model requires substantial domain expertise per process,
careful curation of rate constants, and a runtime cost that does not
trivially shrink with hardware. The result is a small number of
flagship models, each representing years of work, and an
order-of-magnitude gap between the mechanistic detail accessible to
modellers and the catalogue of cells biologists would like to study.

## Single-cell transcriptomics foundation models

The second paradigm replaces mechanism with learned representation. A
transformer or related deep architecture is trained on tens to hundreds
of millions of single-cell transcriptomes — Geneformer
[@theodoris2023geneformer] on approximately 30 million cells [TODO
confirm], scGPT [@cui2024scgpt] on approximately 33 million [TODO
confirm], and the most recent CZI TranscriptFormer release on
approximately 112 million cells across 12 species [TODO confirm exact
figure] — and acquires a latent space in which cell type, state, and
perturbation response can be read out by lightweight downstream heads.
Foundation models excel at three tasks: zero- or few-shot cell-type
annotation across heterogeneous tissues, in-silico perturbation
prediction within the regime they have been trained on, and the
generation of representations that improve the data efficiency of
downstream tasks such as drug-target identification. The CZI Virtual
Cells Platform [@bunne2024build] and the recent rBio system
[@czi2025rbio] formalise these capabilities as the operating
infrastructure of an emerging virtual-cell programme.

What this paradigm does well is the breadth that mechanism cannot
afford: a foundation model trained on tens of millions of cells will
have *seen* far more biological context than any mechanistic model
will ever encode. What it does poorly is the kind of behaviour that
is not in the training distribution: cells with limited single-cell
representation, dynamics that unfold on timescales shorter than the
transcriptional snapshot, and any setting where the question is
*why* something happens rather than *what* happens. Validation is
benchmark-driven and intrinsically retrospective: a foundation model
that has not been measured against a held-out perturbation cannot be
trusted on it. The computational footprint is concentrated in
training rather than inference; inference is cheap, training is at
the limit of what academic groups can afford.

## Hybrid and causal-mechanistic ML

A third paradigm — hybrid in the strict sense — augments mechanistic
models with learned components, or constrains learned models with
mechanistic structure. The Valence Labs position paper [@valence2025]
frames this as *predict, explain, discover*: prediction from learning,
explanation from mechanism, discovery from the interaction. Concrete
instances include physics-informed neural networks for ODE systems,
neural ODE surrogates that approximate the output of expensive
mechanistic simulations at a fraction of the runtime cost, and
mechanism-anchored language models that use a kinetic-model black box
as either a training signal or a verifier (the rBio approach
[@czi2025rbio]). The "LLMs Meet Virtual Cell" survey [@llmsmvc2025]
catalogues a growing list of such hybrids.

What hybrid approaches do well is to address the failures of either
pure paradigm: AI provides scale and pattern-recognition that
mechanism cannot match; mechanism provides causal interpretability and
out-of-distribution validity that pure learning cannot. What they do
poorly is methodology — the field is young, the engineering norms are
unsettled, and the validation regimes for hybrids are an open problem.
Computational footprints span the full range, from neural ODE
surrogates that are cheaper at inference than the simulation they
replace, through to physics-informed networks whose training cost
matches that of the foundation models themselves.

# A taxonomy by cell biology and question

A paradigm choice can be located along three axes that together
capture the practical trade-off space: *mechanism explicitness*, the
degree to which the model's behaviour can be traced to identifiable
biological reactions; *data dependence*, the volume and density of
empirical observations required to fit the model; and *interpretability
requirement*, the extent to which the user requires the model's
predictions to come with a causal account. Figure 1 sketches this space
and locates each paradigm in it.

These axes are not orthogonal in practice, but they map cleanly onto
the questions a working biologist actually asks. *What state is this
cell in?* is a classification question well-served by foundation
models. *How will this perturbation propagate?* spans both paradigms
depending on whether the perturbation is in or out of the training
distribution. *Why does this cell make this decision?* is a causal
question that only mechanism can answer cleanly, however much
correlation a learned model uncovers. And — the question we develop in
the next section — *what does this cell do on a sub-minute timescale
when its transcriptional state is mute?* is a question for which
mechanism is not just preferable but necessary.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\fbox{\begin{minipage}{0.92\linewidth}\centering\vspace{1ex}
\textbf{Figure 1 sketch.} Paradigm placement along three axes:\\[0.5ex]
\textit{mechanism explicitness} (low $\rightarrow$ high), \textit{data dependence} (low $\rightarrow$ high), \textit{interpretability requirement} (low $\rightarrow$ high). \\[0.5ex]
Whole-cell mechanistic models live at the high-mechanism, low-data, high-interpretability corner. \\
Transcriptomics foundation models live at the low-mechanism, high-data, low-interpretability corner. \\
Hybrid approaches occupy the interior diagonal.
\vspace{1ex}
\end{minipage}}
\caption{\textbf{Paradigm classification by cell biology and question.}
The three modelling paradigms are placed in a three-axis space defined
by mechanism explicitness, data dependence, and interpretability
requirement. The axes capture the practical trade-offs that drive
paradigm selection in working cell biology. (Final figure: TikZ /
mermaid sketch to replace the placeholder box.)}
\label{fig:taxonomy}
\end{figure}

# The platelet as a worked example

The human platelet is the cell that breaks the dominant paradigm. It
is anucleate — derived as a cytoplasmic fragment from a megakaryocyte,
with no genome of its own and a residual, non-canonical transcriptome
inherited at the moment of release [TODO confirm best citation for
platelet transcriptome residuality; candidates include Rowley et al.
2011, Schubert et al. 2014]. It is terminally differentiated, with a
circulating half-life of approximately nine days and no division. Its
defining functional behaviour — adhesion, activation, granule
secretion, aggregation, and procoagulant transformation — unfolds on
timescales of seconds to minutes after vascular injury, governed by
calcium signalling, integrin reorganisation, and granule release rather
than by changes in mRNA abundance. And it is poorly represented in
public single-cell transcriptomic atlases relative to its abundance in
the bloodstream, in part because of methodological difficulties in
recovering anucleate cells in standard droplet-based workflows.

Each of these properties closes off a different affordance of the
transcriptomics-foundation-model paradigm. *Anucleate*: there is no
ongoing transcriptional response to model, only a fixed residual mRNA
pool that does not change in any informative way on the timescale of
activation. *Terminally differentiated*: the perturbation-response
benchmarks that foundation models are validated against, where the
response is mediated by transcriptional reprogramming, are not the
biology of interest. *Sub-minute dynamics*: the model time-resolution
required to capture calcium transients, integrin inside-out
signalling, and granule release is incompatible with single-cell
transcriptomic measurement. *Sparse data*: even where one would like
a learned representation, the volume of single-platelet data required
to fit a foundation model is not available, and may not be obtainable
with current technology.

By contrast, mechanistic modelling is well-suited to all four
properties. The platelet whole-cell model under construction in our
group [TODO add citation / link to repository / preprint if available]
extends the wcEcoli engine to a single platelet, with explicit
processes for calcium dynamics, granule secretion, integrin activation,
and metabolism, and explicit compartments for the cytosol, the dense
tubular system (the platelet's calcium store), and the dense and alpha
granules. Validation targets are biophysical: the cytosolic calcium
transient measured by @dolan2014platelet under thrombin / SFLLRN
stimulation; the steady-state ATP turnover measured by @holmsen [TODO
confirm citation, 1979 or 1981]; the SOCE flux under EDTA / no-Ca²⁺
conditions; the calcium-pump and IP3-receptor activity reported in the
detailed kinetic literature [@purvis2008molecular]. None of these
targets is the sort of data foundation models are built to consume.

A hybrid approach has a role to play in this setting, but only after
the mechanistic scaffold is in place. Learned components could supply
priors for poorly-constrained rate constants — and the proteomic
correlation observed by @balabin2024personalization between SERCA and
IP3R copy numbers across donors is exactly the kind of empirical
relationship that a small learned model might encode as a structured
prior. ML surrogates could accelerate the calcium-signalling ODE for
ensemble simulation, particularly important once stochastic effects in
the low-copy receptor cascade are added (a regime in which one wishes
to simulate hundreds of platelet replicates rather than one). None of
this displaces the mechanistic core; it amplifies it.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\fbox{\begin{minipage}{0.92\linewidth}\centering\vspace{1ex}
\textbf{Figure 3 sketch.} Platelet case study, paradigm fit by question. \\[0.5ex]
Rows: (i) transcriptomic state, (ii) perturbation response, (iii) Ca\textsuperscript{2+} dynamics, (iv) granule secretion, (v) integrin activation. \\
Columns: foundation-model fit, hybrid fit, mechanistic fit. \\
Shading: green = paradigm well-suited; amber = partial; red = paradigm cannot address. \\[0.5ex]
Expected pattern: rows (iii)--(v) red under foundation model, amber under hybrid, green under mechanistic.
\vspace{1ex}
\end{minipage}}
\caption{\textbf{Platelet case study by paradigm.} Matrix of platelet
biological questions against modelling paradigm, with paradigm fit
shaded by suitability. The mechanistic column dominates on the
biophysical-dynamics rows; the foundation-model column collapses
because the cell's defining biology is not transcriptional. (Final
figure: full matrix with literature anchors per cell to be drafted.)}
\label{fig:platelet}
\end{figure}

# A software-engineering lens

A complementary view of the same trade-offs comes from software
engineering rather than biology, and it is a view the AIVC literature
has under-developed. A mechanistic whole-cell model is, structurally,
a large, typed, contract-bound piece of software. Every reaction has a
named rate constant, every rate constant has a source citation, every
process advertises its molecular requests in a partition contract, and
every state variable is identifiable in the simulation output by
compartment-tagged species name. This is the *sim_data* discipline of
the wcEcoli lineage [@macklin2020], and it imposes a non-trivial cost:
each new process is a code review, a citation audit, and an
integration test against the rest of the model. The payoff is that any
simulated behaviour is interpretable in the language of the underlying
biology, and any disagreement with experiment is locatable in a
specific rate constant or process.

A foundation model is structurally different. The model is a set of
weights; the *sim_data* equivalent is the training corpus; the contract
is statistical rather than typed. Reproducibility costs lie not in
code review but in dataset versioning and seed management. Validation
costs are concentrated in benchmark curation rather than mechanism
audit. Modularity, where it exists, is the modularity of LoRA
adapters and downstream heads rather than the modularity of cellular
processes. None of this is wrong — it is the appropriate engineering
posture for a model whose value derives from the breadth of training
data — but it means that the kinds of guarantees one can extract from
a foundation model are statistically scoped in a way that a
mechanistic model's guarantees are not.

This distinction matters when the use case is regulatory, clinical, or
adversarial. A pharmaceutical perturbation prediction that comes with
an interpretable causal account of *why* the drug affects the cell
this way is a defensible artefact in a way that a predicted
transcriptomic shift, however accurate, is not. The Karr 2012 model
demonstrated this stance with a gene-essentiality screen
[@karr2012] in which every essentiality call was traceable to a
specific process — a property no foundation-model benchmark currently
delivers.

# Convergence: priors, training signal, surrogates

The framing of paradigm choice as a binary is misleading; the
practically interesting question is how the paradigms compose. Three
specific convergences are already legible in the recent literature and
are the natural near-term scope for any platelet-modelling or
adjacent-cell programme.

First, *AI models as priors for mechanistic parameters*. A
mechanistic whole-cell model has hundreds to thousands of rate
constants, many of them constrained only weakly by experiment. A
learned model trained on proteomic, transcriptomic, and pathway
databases is in a position to supply structured priors over those rate
constants — encoding, for instance, the cross-cell-line correlations
that @balabin2024personalization observe between SERCA and IP3R copy
numbers, or the broader pump-receptor stoichiometric patterns that
proteomic atlases now make visible. The mechanistic model remains the
object of inference; the prior is the contribution.

Second, *mechanistic models as training signal*. The rBio approach
[@czi2025rbio] uses kinetic models as a verifier for a large language
model's perturbation-response predictions: predictions that are
inconsistent with mechanism are penalised. The pattern generalises.
Any mechanistic model fast enough to evaluate at training time can
serve as a constraint on a learned model, narrowing the hypothesis
class to mechanistically defensible regions.

Third, *AI surrogates for accelerated mechanistic simulation*. A
neural ODE or transformer surrogate trained on the outputs of a
mechanistic simulation can deliver inference-time speeds two to four
orders of magnitude faster than the underlying integrator, at the
cost of out-of-distribution validity. For ensemble simulation —
indispensable once stochasticity is added to a low-copy signalling
cascade — this is the difference between a single replicate per
hour and a thousand. Figure 2 sketches the decision space.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\fbox{\begin{minipage}{0.92\linewidth}\centering\vspace{1ex}
\textbf{Figure 2 sketch.} Decision matrix for paradigm selection. \\[0.5ex]
Rows: (i) cell biology has rich single-cell data; (ii) cell biology has sub-minute dynamics; (iii) question is causal-explanatory; (iv) question is perturbation-prediction within training distribution; (v) regulatory / clinical interpretability required. \\
Columns: foundation model recommended; hybrid recommended; mechanistic recommended. \\[0.5ex]
A clinician answering (i) no, (ii) yes, (iii) yes, (iv) no, (v) yes lands squarely in the mechanistic column.
\vspace{1ex}
\end{minipage}}
\caption{\textbf{Decision matrix for paradigm selection.} Cell-biology
and question-type axes against recommended paradigm. The matrix is
deliberately coarse: it expresses where each paradigm is well-suited,
not a quantitative model-selection procedure. (Final figure: full
matrix with literature exemplars per cell to be drafted.)}
\label{fig:decision}
\end{figure}

# Conclusion

The argument of this Perspective is not that the AI virtual cell is a
mistake, nor that mechanistic whole-cell modelling is a recovered
orthodoxy. It is that the choice between them — and the choice of any
hybrid between them — is properly made on the basis of the cell and
the question, not on the basis of technology momentum. The human
platelet is one cell among many for which the dominant paradigm's
assumptions break, and for which a mechanistic and hybrid stance is
forced by biology. Other cells — post-mitotic neurons,
cardiomyocytes, erythrocytes, terminal effector lymphocytes — pose
their own variants of the same problem. Foundation models, mechanistic
models, and hybrids each have a clear remit; that remit is determined
by the data, the dynamics, the interpretability requirement, and the
question, not by which paradigm has the largest current grant
portfolio. We close, deliberately, with no methodological consensus
beyond that: fit the paradigm to the cell.

# Acknowledgements

The platelet whole-cell modelling work that informs the worked example
is conducted by the author at the University of Reading; the wcEcoli
engine on which it builds is the work of the Covert lab at Stanford
[@macklin2020] and the Karr group, originally at Mt Sinai
[@karr2012]. The framing of cell-biology-first paradigm selection has
benefited from conversations with TODO. Funding TODO.

# Author contributions

S.H. conceived the argument and drafted the manuscript. M.F. TODO.

# Conflict of interest

The authors declare no conflicts of interest.
